companies.name: ADP
companies.model: Diversified payroll & HR platform — benefits administration is one line among many

companies.name: WEX Inc.
companies.model: Payments company with a benefits-accounts segment (HSA/FSA/COBRA)

companies.name: Alight Solutions
companies.model: HR & benefits outsourcing at enterprise scale
companies.note: Divested its Payroll & Professional Services business to H.I.G. Capital (now "Strada") for up to $1.2B in mid-2024 — now a purer benefits-admin play than before.

companies.name: HealthEquity
companies.model: HSA custodian and consumer-directed benefits administrator, incl. COBRA

companies.name: TriNet Group
companies.model: PEO co-employing client staff to deliver outsourced payroll, HR, compliance & benefits (incl. COBRA)
companies.note: Revenue shown is "professional service revenue," the figure comparable to the others — TriNet's total GAAP revenue is ~$5.0B, but ~$4.28B of that is pass-through insurance premiums, not retained revenue.

companies.name: Empyrean Benefit Solutions
companies.model: Benefits administration & enrollment tech for large employers, brokers, and private exchanges, incl. COBRA
companies.note: Moderate-confidence estimate — Empyrean doesn't file separate financials since its 2019 acquisition by Securian.

mnaDeals.sig: The deal that kicked off the current consolidation wave — took a public benefits-admin SaaS platform private under an insurer's ownership.

mnaDeals.deal: HealthEquity acquired the BenefitWallet HSA portfolio from Conduent
mnaDeals.sig: Added ~616,000 HSAs and $2.7B in HSA assets, pushing HealthEquity past 9 million HSA members — direct consolidation at the HSA-custodian tier.
Bolted on employee-benefits outsourcing, risk management, and compliance capability.

mnaDeals.deal: Navia Benefit Solutions acquired Nexus Administrators
mnaDeals.sig: Continued Navia's roll-up strategy in the regional COBRA/FSA/HSA TPA space.

mnaDeals.deal: H.I.G. Capital ("Strada") acquired Alight's Payroll & Professional Services business
mnaDeals.sig: Major structural event — Alight shed its payroll/HCM-outsourcing arm to refocus purely on benefits administration, which is why its post-2024 revenue and headcount (shown above) are smaller than before.
A smaller bolt-on (internal communications/engagement firm) by another major benefits-admin platform player.

mnaDeals.deal: Navia Benefit Solutions acquired Flex-Plan Retirement Services
mnaDeals.sig: Navia expanded from health/FSA/COBRA into retirement-plan TPA services — cross-category consolidation.

mnaDeals.deal: Businessolver acquired the assets of ProView Global
mnaDeals.sig: Brought an existing back-office/BPO partner in-house, expanding 24/7 administrative support — TPAs vertically integrating service delivery rather than outsourcing it.

forces.name: Competitive Rivalry
forces.text: Rivalry is intense — five real competitors plus regional players are chasing the same mid-market employers, and M&A has become a routine growth tactic rather than an exception (7 deals in 3 years, above).

forces.name: Threat of New Entrants
forces.text: Compliance complexity (COBRA/ACA/multi-state law) and platform-build costs are real barriers, but PE-backed roll-ups like Navia and OneDigital show capital — not technology — is often the actual key to entry.

forces.name: Buyer Power (employers)
forces.text: Employers can and do switch TPAs, and brokers exist specifically to shop the market for them — but switching costs rise fast once a client is embedded in a specific tech platform, which is exactly why platforms compete on lock-in as much as price.

forces.name: Supplier Power
forces.text: The 'suppliers' here are mostly insurance carriers and banking partners for HSA custody — fragmented enough that no single one holds real pricing power over administrators.

forces.name: Threat of Substitutes
forces.text: PEOs like TriNet bundle benefits administration into a broader co-employment model, and in-house HR software is increasingly absorbing the basic admin functions that used to require a dedicated TPA.
